content.text: Status Page Update Cadence

The status page is the primary interface between your engineering team and the users, customers, and stakeholders waiting for resolution. A status page that goes silent for 40 minutes during a SEV-1 incident does not communicate competence—it communicates disorganization. Cadence discipline is not a communications nicety; it is a trust-maintenance mechanism that directly influences customer retention and regulatory perception.
Cadence by Severity Level

attrs.alt: Status page update cadence table by severity level

content.text: What Every Update Must Include

A status page update that says only 'We are investigating' after 30 minutes provides no value. Each update must answer three questions: what is currently affected, what the team is doing about it, and when the next update will arrive.

What is affected: be specific about which features, endpoints, or user populations are impacted. 'AI assistant responses may be slower than usual' is better than 'service degradation'. 'Users in the EU region may experience elevated error rates' is better than 'some users are affected'.

What we are doing: describe the action in plain language. 'Our team is investigating the root cause' is the minimum acceptable. 'We have identified that the issue is related to our LLM provider and are switching to our backup provider' is better.

When the next update arrives: always include a time-bounded commitment. 'We will provide an update by 10:30 UTC' is stronger than 'We will update you shortly'. If the cadence will be 15 minutes, say so explicitly.
Update Templates by Phase

# Status Page Update Templates

## Opening Update (T+0 to T+5)
### Investigating: {Component Name} Degradation
We are investigating reports of {description of impact} affecting {scope of users}.

Our team is currently identifying the root cause.

We will provide an update by {T + cadence_interval} UTC.

---

## In-Progress Update (mid-incident, when cause known)
### Identified: {Component Name} Issue
We have identified that {plain-language root cause description}.

Our team is currently {specific mitigation action in progress}.

Some users may experience {specific impact description}.

We will provide an update by {next update time} UTC.

---

## Monitoring Update (mitigation deployed, watching)
### Monitoring: {Component Name} Recovery
We have deployed a fix for {root cause description}.

We are monitoring the system to confirm full recovery. Error rates are
currently {X}% and declining.

Affected users may need to {retry / refresh} to see improvement.

We will provide a final update by {next update time} UTC.

---

## Resolution Update
### Resolved: {Component Name} Incident
The issue affecting {component} has been resolved.

**Impact:** {N} users experienced {description of impact} for {duration}.

**Root cause:** {plain-language summary}.

**Fix:** {what was done}.

We apologize for the disruption. A detailed postmortem will be published
within {48/72/5 business days based on severity}.

---

## Post-Resolution Postmortem Reference
### Postmortem Published: {Incident ID}
We have published a postmortem for the {date} incident affecting {component}.

Read the full analysis and corrective actions at: {postmortem URL}
Automation: Scheduled Status Page Reminders

The IC under pressure will forget to post status page updates. A simple scheduled Slack reminder in the incident channel, triggered at the cadence interval, ensures no update is missed without requiring the IC to track time.

"""status_page_cadence.py -- cadence reminder scheduler for incident channels."""
import os
import time
import threading
from datetime import datetime, timezone
import httpx

SLACK_TOKEN = os.environ["SLACK_BOT_TOKEN"]

CONFIG = {
    "SEV-1": {"interval_minutes": 15, "requires_update": True},
    "SEV-2": {"interval_minutes": 30, "requires_update": True},
    "SEV-3": {"interval_minutes": 60, "requires_update": False},
    "SEV-4": {"interval_minutes": 0, "requires_update": False},  # 0 = no reminders
}


def start_cadence_reminder(
    channel_id: str,
    severity: str,
    statuspage_update_url: str,
) -> threading.Event:
    """Start a background thread posting cadence reminders.

    Returns a stop_event — call stop_event.set() to stop reminders when
    the incident is resolved.
    """
    config = CONFIG.get(severity, {})
    interval = config.get("interval_minutes", 0)
    if interval == 0:
        return threading.Event()  # No-op for SEV-4

    stop_event = threading.Event()

    def _reminder_loop():
        next_reminder = time.time() + interval * 60
        while not stop_event.wait(timeout=max(0, next_reminder - time.time())):
            now = datetime.now(timezone.utc).strftime("%H:%M UTC")
            msg = (
                f":rotating_light: *Status Page Update Due*\n"
                f"Time: {now}\n"
                f"Cadence: every {interval} minutes (SEV: {severity})\n"
                f"Update at: {statuspage_update_url}\n"
                f"Template: #inc-templates pinned message"
            )
            with httpx.Client() as client:
                client.post(
                    "https://slack.com/api/chat.postMessage",
                    headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {SLACK_TOKEN}"},
                    json={"channel": channel_id, "text": msg},
                )
            next_reminder = time.time() + interval * 60

    thread = threading.Thread(target=_reminder_loop, daemon=True)
    thread.start()
    return stop_event


# Example usage:
# stop = start_cadence_reminder(
#     channel_id="C12345678",
#     severity="SEV-1",
#     statuspage_update_url="https://manage.statuspage.io/pages/xxxxx",
# )
# ... incident resolves ...
# stop.set()  # stop reminders
AI-Specific Language Guidance for Status Updates

AI service incidents require careful language choices on the status page. Unlike infrastructure outages—where 'service unavailable' is unambiguous—AI capability failures often produce partial or degraded responses that are hard to describe accurately without either alarming users unnecessarily or understating the impact.
Incident Type
Avoid
Use Instead
Hallucination
"AI is hallucinating"
"AI assistant responses may be less accurate than usual"
Safety violation
"AI produced harmful content"
"We identified responses that did not meet our content standards"
Rate limit
"API quota exceeded"
"AI features may be temporarily unavailable or slower"
Reasoning loop
"AI is stuck in a loop"
"Some AI tasks may take longer than usual or timeout"
Status Page Component Mapping

Statuspage.io and similar tools organize updates by component. Poorly organized components produce updates that say 'Service Degraded' without helping users determine whether their specific workflow is affected. Component design for agentic AI services requires translating internal technical boundaries into user-facing capability groupings.

Map components to user-visible capabilities, not internal services. 'AI Chat Assistant', 'Document Analysis', and 'Automated Task Execution' are better component names than 'llm-inference-service', 'vector-retrieval', and 'tool-executor'.

Create sub-components for regional availability if your service is deployed across geographies. A rate limit in one LLM provider region may affect only EU users; the status page should reflect that specificity.

Include a 'Platform Infrastructure' component that maps to shared services (API gateway, authentication). This allows you to indicate infrastructure degradation without implying AI capability failure.

Use the 'under maintenance' status for planned work. Scheduled maintenance windows for model upgrades, prompt deployments, or infrastructure changes should appear on the status page 48 hours in advance.
Statuspage.io API Integration

Manually updating the status page during an incident costs 2-3 minutes per update and risks errors under pressure. Integrate the Statuspage.io API into your incident automation so the IC can post updates via Slack command or the war room setup script handles the initial update automatically.

"""statuspage_client.py -- Statuspage.io API wrapper for incident updates."""
import os
import httpx
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional, Literal


STATUSPAGE_API_KEY = os.environ["STATUSPAGE_API_KEY"]
PAGE_ID = os.environ["STATUSPAGE_PAGE_ID"]
BASE_URL = "https://api.statuspage.io/v1"

ComponentStatus = Literal[
    "operational",
    "degraded_performance",
    "partial_outage",
    "major_outage",
    "under_maintenance",
]

IncidentStatus = Literal[
    "investigating",
    "identified",
    "monitoring",
    "resolved",
]


@dataclass
class IncidentUpdate:
    status: IncidentStatus
    body: str
    affected_component_id: str
    affected_component_status: ComponentStatus


class StatuspageClient:
    def __init__(self):
        self.session = httpx.Client(
            headers={
                "Authorization": f"OAuth {STATUSPAGE_API_KEY}",
                "Content-Type": "application/json",
            },
            base_url=BASE_URL,
            timeout=10,
        )

    def create_incident(
        self,
        title: str,
        body: str,
        component_id: str,
        component_status: ComponentStatus,
        incident_status: IncidentStatus = "investigating",
        deliver_notifications: bool = True,
    ) -> str:
        """Create a new incident. Returns incident_id."""
        resp = self.session.post(
            f"/pages/{PAGE_ID}/incidents",
            json={
                "incident": {
                    "name": title,
                    "status": incident_status,
                    "body": body,
                    "deliver_notifications": deliver_notifications,
                    "components": {component_id: component_status},
                    "component_ids": [component_id],
                }
            },
        )
        resp.raise_for_status()
        return resp.json()["id"]

    def post_update(
        self,
        incident_id: str,
        update: IncidentUpdate,
    ) -> None:
        """Post an update to an existing incident."""
        resp = self.session.post(
            f"/pages/{PAGE_ID}/incidents/{incident_id}/incident_updates",
            json={
                "incident_update": {
                    "status": update.status,
                    "body": update.body,
                    "deliver_notifications": True,
                    "affected_components": [
                        {
                            "code": update.affected_component_id,
                            "name": "AI Assistant",  # Display name
                            "old_status": "major_outage",
                            "new_status": update.affected_component_status,
                        }
                    ],
                }
            },
        )
        resp.raise_for_status()

    def resolve_incident(
        self,
        incident_id: str,
        resolution_body: str,
        component_id: str,
    ) -> None:
        """Mark incident resolved and restore component to operational."""
        self.post_update(
            incident_id,
            IncidentUpdate(
                status="resolved",
                body=resolution_body,
                affected_component_id=component_id,
                affected_component_status="operational",
            ),
        )

    def close(self) -> None:
        self.session.close()
Subscriber Notification Strategy

Status page subscribers who receive email or SMS notifications for every component update will unsubscribe after a few noisy incidents. The notification strategy must balance comprehensive disclosure with subscriber fatigue.

Deliver subscriber notifications for the initial incident declaration and the resolution update. Suppress notifications for intermediate updates unless the status changes (e.g., 'investigating' to 'identified' or 'monitoring').
For SEV-1 incidents, deliver all updates with notifications. Subscribers of a SEV-1 have a legitimate need for every status change.
Provide component-level subscription so enterprise customers can subscribe only to the AI Assistant component and not receive infrastructure maintenance updates that do not affect their workflow.

For enterprise accounts affected by a SEV-1 or SEV-2, supplement the public status page with a direct email from the Customer Success team within the first 30 minutes. The status page is not a substitute for direct enterprise account communication.
Retrospective Review of Update Quality

Status page update quality degrades over time if it is not reviewed systematically. Add a section to your monthly incident review that grades the previous month's status page updates against the three-question standard (what is affected, what are we doing, when is the next update). Teams that review their status page updates improve the quality of subsequent updates noticeably.

Grade your status page updates against these three tests: (1) Could a user determine whether their specific workflow is affected from this update? (2) Does the update say what the team is doing, not just that they are investigating? (3) Does the update give a specific time for the next update, not 'shortly'? If any test fails, the update template and the scribe training need revision.
Key Takeaways

Cadence discipline is a trust mechanism. Missing a committed update time communicates disorganization more loudly than the incident itself. Automate cadence reminders to the IC so updates are never forgotten.
Every update must answer three questions: what is affected, what is being done, and when the next update will arrive. 'Still investigating' alone is not an acceptable update after the first report.
Use incident type-specific language templates to avoid AI-specific jargon ('hallucination', 'model failure') that confuses or alarms users unnecessarily.
Publish a postmortem link from the status page within the timeline committed for each severity. This closes the loop with affected users and demonstrates accountability.
Resolution updates must confirm the mitigation, describe the impact duration and scope, and provide a postmortem ETA. Resolution is not the end of the communication obligation.
